$comment: OB-5 — authored Epoch-0 for a BAVARIA/origins founding (1885). Mirrors the Ireland slice's shape (birth+date → naming → station → schooling → emergent calling) but with distinct Bavarian texture: an Alpine brewing village, the long shadow of Prussian conscription, the orderly Catholic-and-beer-hall life. Place-gated (place:'bavaria'); ev_bav_birth weight 5000 so it always opens; generic ev_birth_generic/calling are notFlags-excluded for place:bavaria (see new-york/origins events.json).

events.title: Born Under the Alps
events.scene: You arrive in a timber-and-plaster house in a valley town where the church bell and the brewery clock keep rival time. Snow sits blue on the peaks even now; below, the smell of woodsmoke and fermenting hops has soaked into the very beams. The midwife works briskly — everything in this country is done briskly — and the doctor, called for the well-off births and yours by luck, opens his ledger and inks the date with Prussian neatness.
events.researchNote: OB-5 Bavaria birth opener. Sets `emerged`; narrates the seed-drawn date via {birth_date}. Alpine brewing town, 1885, recently-unified Germany.

choices.text: You overhear the date he says — the year that fixes when you are.
choices.outcome: “{birth_date},” he writes, and underlines it once. In this country a thing written down is a thing that has happened; you have, officially, begun.

events.title: The Christening Register
events.scene: You are taken to the baroque village church, all gold and trembling candlelight, to be entered in the register beneath the {family_name} name. The priest's pen hovers: a saint's name for protection, a grandfather's for continuity, or the new fashion for a hero of the young Reich. The choice is spoken; you are written down as {full_name}.
events.researchNote: OB-5 Bavaria given-naming. The given name was dealt from the culture pool at founding; this beat is the church-register bestowal that sets `named` and frames the family's posture toward faith, kin, and the new German nation.

choices.text: A saint's name — the Church is older than any empire and will outlast this one too.
choices.outcome: The old faith claims you first. In Bavaria that is never the wrong bet.

choices.text: A grandfather's name — the family is its own small nation.
choices.outcome: An old name, carried like a tool that still has work in it. The dead will be watching how you use it.

choices.text: A name in the new German fashion — the empire is the coming thing.
choices.outcome: The village priest sniffs, but writes it. You are marked, faintly, as a child of the future rather than the parish.

events.title: Order, and Who Keeps It
events.scene: You grow old enough to read the town. There is a way things are done here, and a register for each of them — the brewery's guild, the church's tithe, the garrison's rolls that will one day want your name. Your father tips his hat to the burgomaster and the officer both, and you watch where real authority sits: not with the loudest man, but with the one whose stamp the others wait for.
events.researchNote: OB-5: class/station emerges from observing orderly, hierarchical Bavarian life — guild, church, garrison. Sets `station_seen`; an early anchor the calling reads. Conscription foreshadowed.

choices.text: You learn to love the stamp — order is a ladder, if you climb it.
choices.outcome: Where others see rules, you see rungs. You begin, quietly, to climb.

choices.text: You hate the garrison rolls that will one day claim you — and plan to be unclaimable.
choices.outcome: The state wants your body for its wars. You decide, young, to be worth too much to spend cheaply.

choices.text: You trust the brewery and the guild — honest work, honestly rewarded.
choices.outcome: There is a deep contentment in a craft and a community. Whether contentment founds a dynasty is the open question.

events.title: The Gymnasium Gate
events.scene: Germany schools its children harder than most, and a clever one can rise — if the family can spare him from work and afford the fees. Herr Doktor Wagner, who teaches the Latin and the mathematics, marks you out. "The Reich is built by men who were noticed in rooms like this," he tells you, tapping your copybook. "The only sin is to be ordinary on purpose."
events.researchNote: OB-5: the calling EMERGES via schooling + an ambitious teacher, gated by whether the family can afford the Gymnasium. Sets `schooled`. German meritocratic-rise framing.

choices.text: You master the Latin and the figures — knowledge is the surest ladder.
choices.outcome: Herr Doktor Wagner sets you problems meant for older boys, and you solve them. A path opens that the valley cannot close.

choices.text: You learn to command the other boys — leadership is its own subject.
choices.outcome: You find that others fall in behind you without being asked. In this country, that is a talent the state will pay well for.

choices.text: You leave for the brewery floor — a trade in hand beats a degree in debt.
choices.outcome: Herr Doktor Wagner calls it a waste; your father calls it sense. The vats do not care for Latin, and they always need tending.

events.title: What the New Germany Asks of You
events.scene: You are a young {given_name} {surname} now, and the country around you is hungry, modern, arming. A man must choose what he will be FOR — and in this Germany the choices have edges. You feel the pull harden into a path the family will follow for a century.
events.researchNote: OB-5: the Bavaria calling crystallizes into the archetype (setsArchetype). Same 6-archetype shape as Ireland; German framing (industry, the state, the Church, the laboratory). Replaces the generic calling for bavaria.

choices.text: Industry. You will turn the valley's hops and timber into a fortune.
choices.outcome: The Magnate's road: brewery into cartel, cartel into capital. The line will own the means, and let others mind the labor.

choices.text: The State. You will serve — and steer — the new German power.
choices.outcome: The Statesman's road: the ministry, the party, the rolls you once feared now signed in your own hand. The line will sit close to power, for good and for ill.

choices.text: The Laboratory. You will build the machines the coming century will run on.
choices.outcome: The Visionary's road: German engineering as a kind of faith. The line will make what does not yet exist, and the world will buy it.

choices.text: The Church. You will hold the old faith against the hungry new age.
choices.outcome: The Prophet's road: the pulpit as a power older than any Reich. The line will keep souls when empires fall — and empires, here, will fall.

choices.text: The stage. You have the music in you — the beer-hall song, the operetta, the voice that fills a room and empties a purse.
choices.outcome: The Star's road: the cabaret, the radio, the face the new century cannot look away from. The line will be famous before it is safe — and in this country, fame is a weapon that cuts both ways.

choices.text: The body. You are the strongest in the valley, and the new Reich will worship strength like a god.
choices.outcome: The Champion's road: the gymnastics hall, the stadium, the medals a hungry nation will hang on its heroes. The line will win glory — and learn what glory costs when the state owns the crowd.
